- #MarketingStrategy Compared to competitors you can get pretty much the same product in most industries. The key difference for success? Marketing strategy. Having a great brand and effective promotional strategy.

			- Business Commitment Analysis
			  id:: 644c0a7b-1d57-4b06-8383-02043e23686e
			  #BusinessCommitmentAnalysis
				- Market Research & Strategy
					- Fastlane: 5 Commandments
					  collapsed:: true
					  #Fastlane-NECST
						- 5 NECST Commandments
							- [Need]
							  collapsed:: true
							  #Fastlane-Need
								- Solve problems where people are already paying for solutions to the same problem
								- Evergreen
								- Painful to be without or passionately desired
							- [Entry]
							  collapsed:: true
							  #Fastlane-Entry
								- Why
									- Too much supply (competition) will decrease profits
									- **However** this is counterbalanced by Entry:Need ratio (Supply:Demand)
								- High barrier to entry
									- i.e. some barriers, learning curve
							- [Control] i.e. product or business owner
							  collapsed:: true
							  #Fastlane-Control
								- Overview
									- Pros
										- Usually increases longevity of the income stream
									- Cons
										- Bigger investment than selling other people's products
											- Makes sense to test a market by first selling other people's products before creating my own
								- Product owner/creator
								  #ProductCreation https://workflowy.com/#/c2c4024dcfa0
								- **Not**
									- Owning the audience
									  #AffiliateMarketing
									- Or being the investor running the whole marketing campaign
									- Selling other people's products
									  #SellingExistingProducts https://workflowy.com/#/5d2f693c05ab
							- [Scale]
							  collapsed:: true
							  #Fastlane-Scale
								- Law of Effection
									- Serve in magnitude a few massively
									- or in scale many simultaneously
							- [Time]
							  collapsed:: true
							  #Fastlane-Time
								- Business must be able to be completely automated (through these 5 systems), thus making it time-independent and saleable.
								- Note: May need to plan out the Organisational Hierarchy using LucidChart.
									- No linchpins, all Operations Manuals.
								- Computer systems
									- Can either charge either one-time fee
										- Many internet or software businesses e.g. operating systems, encryption software, anti-virus
									- OR a subscription fee i.e. SaaS
										- Recurring subscription rather than one-time - creates lock-in;
										- e.g. digitally-delivered services with subscription
										- e.g. Dropbox, Market Samurai subsc,
								- Content Systems
									- e.g. Membership sites (though often limited audience); ✓ How to Create & Launch a Digital Product, books, blog, magazines
									- -Content Marketing (using my writers content + PLR content + http://thebestspinner.com + PAs for repetitive engagement)
									- “Catch a man a fish and you can sell it to him. Teach a man to fish, and you’ve ruined a wonderful business opportunity.”
									- i.e. instead create businesses centered around Personal Anonymity; Personal Finance/Minimalism; Become a Transhuman (devices/cyborg etc)
									- Create a platform e.g. interview podcast/video series, forums, Rooshvforum for international players and return of kings for getting your POV across. I can highlight others habit systems eg take many guest posts, members area for them to share their google calendar or monthly reviews
								- Distribution Systems
									- Franchising, chaining, network marketing, and television marketing
									- dollarshaveclub ($1/mo for razor); quarterly.co
									- eCommerce, affiliate marketing
								- Rental Systems
									- Real estate, licenses, and patents
								- Human Resource Systems
									- Opt for monthly fee (Productised Service business) rather than one-time (per project or hour i.e. Digital Project Management-style)
					- [Target Market] the Masses or the 1%
					  ((644c0a7b-82fa-4930-a46f-7dad1e537201))

				- Long-Term Effects
					- Minimise Hassles
						- No location dependence
						- No inventory
						  collapsed:: true
							- Inventory can be a hassle, but more Control and probably higher profit than dropshipping
							- No Inventory types
								- Affiliate marketing
								- Dropshipping
								- Non-physical products
									- Info
									- Software
									- Membership
						- No taxes
						  collapsed:: true
							- Ideally using Bitcoin
							- Other methods can be very low tax though eg get a merchant account in Bulgaria
						- Operations Manual for every employee - no linchpins
						- Skill-based rather than Creative
						  collapsed:: true
							- Pros/Cons
								- Pros
									- It's hard and being creative for an extended period of time is even much harder. Most people overestimate their ability to sustain creativity, even if it is something as "easy" as writing a daily blog. Creativity burnout is real for many people, which is why most people can't and won't be location independent through creative means.
								- Cons
									- Creativity is viable if you have a backlog of ideas and account for the slow pace of creativity
										- That's why I approach it with the expectation that it will happen at it's own speed. I just maintain a product testing regiment and allow the ideas to come to me when they want to. Due to the slow pace of product testing, I always have a backlog of ideas to work on when I'm in the mood to create, when I'm not, I just rest up or go out to enjoy some socializing or otherwise keep myself healthy.
									- Skill-based is more easily automated by robots/AI
							- Suits' examples
								- Engineers, programmers, artists, product designers, writers, etc. all have to use different methods to will nothing into something.
								- These careers all combine a degree of skill and creativity. Some people in these fields lean heavily towards the skill side and only a small number really engage in creativity as a large part of their job. For example, an engineer that spends his days crunching the numbers to ensure that the next bridge his team builds doesn't collapse isn't creating anything new. He's putting his knowledge and skills to use to help with the building of a fairly routine structure.
								- My brother is an electrical engineer and I'd estimate that his work is about 5% creative. The other 95% is participating in meetings (to share from his knowledge of what is possible and impossible, recommended and not recommended, affordable and cost-prohibitive)putting the actual pieces of a design together and then testing finished products to make sure that they work. Only a small portion of his work day goes into devising solutions that are not repetitive and routine in nature.
								- Product designers are generally going to fall much more into the creative zone for their day to day work, which is why you'll rarely meet a professional product designer.
								- Product design usually involves investing a ton of time and usually money into something that is completely unproven.
								- Of the companies that are large enough to sustain this type of development, this work is generally done through their R&D department, which I'm guessing mostly employees people who are there to practice a specific skill, not create. They'll spend a lot of time research potential market demands, testing ideas out, and building prototypes, which are all skills more so than creative functions.
								- I'm guessing that even in a big company, there are at best only a handful of people who are actually tasked with thinking about the next big idea (if any at all). I think it's far more likely for a company to empower people who come up with an idea (despite what their real job tasks are) and help them develop that idea than specifically assign people to trying to think something up, as creativity tends to happen on its own schedule and is hard to do as a job.
								- Artists, if they are creating logos, for example, a high degree of creativity goes into their work, as each product needs to express a certain uniqueness. Watercolour artists, on the other hand, practice the skill of turning a real world scene in a finished painting. Not much creativity there. It's 99% skill.
								- Writing is also more of a skill than a creative function, especially if doing non-fiction. Good fiction will be good because of the ability of the writer to create a story that doesn't bore you because it's new and therefore interesting.
								- Non-fiction is far more about practicing the skill of doing good research and then clearly communicating concepts and information concisely.
							- McDonalds examples
								- Speedee Service System - how to structure a fast food place for efficiency
								  https://youtu.be/jTageuhPfAM
								- I love the film The Founder because it shows a real creative process better than any movie I've ever watched. Two brothers set out to create a highly efficient kitchen service.
								- This is what creativity looks like. Notice, that unlike slapping watercolours on a canvass, it's a process of trial and error. You don't exactly go into it with a clear solution in mind. Rather, you come into it with a problem and you test hypotheses as to what could be a possible solution.
								- This particular scene shows a 6 hour process, which I would consider a very quick turn round time. The solutions I work on involve months of turn around from idea, through prototype building and testing, which is a multi-cycle process unless you get it perfect the first time. The creative process for many things can stretch out much longer, years or even decades.
								- There are two more points that stand out from that film, but the first contains spoilers, so only read the bold if you've already seen the film or don't care about spoilers.
								- In the film (and the real life story on which it is based), the man who is ultimately successful is not a creative person. He's simply a man capable of recognizing a good idea when he sees one. You see several times in the film that other people bring him new ideas that he chooses to implement, but you never see him invent anything himself.
								- The creative guys who came up with the business concept that Kroc turns into the biggest fast-food chain in the world got screwed in the end because while they had the creative ability, they lacked the drive and business savvy to grow their small business into a national chain and couldn't handle the stress of fighting to maintain control of the business they'd poured all their passion into.
								- The second point is that the McDonalds business went against many business assumptions throughout it's history. Instead of introducing better, more comfortable chairs, they actually innovated uncomfortable chairs to reduce the time people stayed camped out at a table.
								- In reference to the point above about creating a coffee shop that is better run and more responsive to customer needs than its competition, sometimes the money is in not responding to customer needs (at least not entirely), but rather finding ways to minimize costs while retaining customers. The drive-through window is genius, as it allows restaurants to sell food at regular prices without having to pay rent for a larger dining area or delivery drivers and allows fast-food joints to continue selling at a point in the evening where it's unprofitable to keep the dining room open (but still profitable to keep the kitchen open).
								- It's a perfect example of going against the grain (taking away the dining room experience and table service from customers), but making more money because the benefits being able to pick up dinner quickly on the way home was something that kept customers coming back, despite the departure from conventional wisdom on how to run a restaurant.
								- Tim Hortons in Canada has been very successful by offering a drive-through window as a coffee shop, since coffee costs almost nothing to brew and a coffee-shop's biggest liability is customers who sip a $1 coffee for 5 hours while they take up valuable real estate inside the venue.
								- There's a reason why there are far more Tim Horton locations in Canada than Starbucks. It's not because people don't like $7 sugar bombs. It's because Tim Horton's has a superior business model (in my opinion).

					- ((649b11ec-b96e-4911-97e2-3a9f4fdcddc7))
					- [Long-Term Gain]
						- Type of Gain
						  collapsed:: true
							- Two aspects
								- If the business failed tomorrow, what would I gain?
								- If the business was successful but I wanted to move on, what have I gained?
							- Priority order of value
								- Equity
								  Only relevant to startups using venture capital
								- ((644c0a7a-4f97-4923-9284-8ad612975a29))
								- Customer list
								- Ownership of Product (Control)
								  #ProductCreation https://workflowy.com/#/c2c4024dcfa0
								- Generates monthly residual income
								- Brand
								  With high traffic, social shares etc
								- Operations Manuals
								- Skills
								  #BusinessCommitmentAnalysis-Skills https://dynalist.io/d/mXC8LQ8sDMg7vNFI62mDSXJI#z=mfwlor20A0EaZ_wlO-1KVgVY
								- Market knowledge
							- Cedonulli: _One has to be either extremely good at the technical aspects of the marketing / funnel, or be super set on building a proper brand._
							- Does this result in an asset which can later on be sold?
						- Lifestyle Gain
						  collapsed:: true
							- #1 - Does it harness the skills I want to master?:
							- #2 - In 5 years time what would my life be like?
							- What is the passive income ceiling?:
							- ((644c0b2d-db30-4240-883d-fc0cab6c9614))
					- Leverage & Develop Strengths
					  id:: 644c0a7b-9349-4a47-a798-16da358f6499
						- **Why**
						  collapsed:: true
							- Know your strengths + weaknesses
							  collapsed:: true
								- eg there are many different types of digital marketing specialists
									- Within the digital marketing industry, there are SEO specialists, optimization experts (site, campaign, or monetization), affiliate marketers, shit peddlers aka gurus, jv brokers, content marketers, CPA network owners, , site flippers, site generators, project managers. web development, product design/development, lead generation,, media buying, CPC advertising, the list goes on.
								- Specialize in one thing, outsource the rest
									- Considering how diverse the world has gone, it is pretty much impossible to be even “ok” at everything. Even when it comes to internet marketing, between all of different types of #Promotion and #ProductCreation, you just can not be good at them all.
									- But I have a good news for you. In order to be successful you simply need to be good at one of them and reach the top 10% of the population for that one skill.
									- The reason I tell you to reach the top 10% of any skill is because the top 10% of any population tends to earn 3-5x more than the top 50%. Real revenue comes when you’re close to the top. If you can ever reach the top 5% of even 1%, you will literally earn 10-20x more than the top 50%.
									- Example from a SEO guy
									  collapsed:: true
										- Let’s take a personal example once again. I didn’t just focus on SEO, I focused on white hat link building (and quite a bit of technical SEO). I focused hard on it and as a consequence, my guest post service is literally close to making 7 figures a year serving the biggest brands out there!
									- Example: a business strategists plan
									  collapsed:: true
										- For me personally, I find the challenge of creating new things enticing. I am always up for building the sites, coming up with ideas and creating them. I like to create strategies and plans of attack. I have dozens of power points of link diagrams. I have spreadsheets of BST providers that I would pair together to test shit. I have spreadsheets of anchor texts for my niches. I am a planner, a strategist, a general. However, I am never going to fight the war in the trenches! That’s just not me. Been there, done that. Hated it! Refuse to do it again!
										- So, my plan is taking shape. What I will do.
											- I will create the initial site
											- I will develop a plan for building links
											- I will develop a content promotion strategy
											- I will create the categories and topics.
											- I will be the vision.
										- What I won’t do.
											- I won’t create content
											- I won’t build links
											- I won’t do social
											- I won’t do relationship marketing
											- I won’t work in the trenches
											- I won't do design or coding
								- Sometimes this may be an excuse for procrastination - everyone has a "great business idea"
							- Comparative advantage
							  collapsed:: true
								- What do I have that other people do not have
									- Something I can be in top 1%, not just top 20% for
								- and how do I get the most out of it
								- Don't jump around from skill to skill - focus and leverage one for all it's worth
							- Follow Your Skills Not Your Dreams
							  source:: http://wallstreetplayboys.com/follow-your-skills-not-your-dreams/
							  collapsed:: true
								- 1) Following your skills will lead you as *close as possible* to your actual dream
									- Go for something you can be in the top 1% for, not top 20%
									- 2) People who argue that it is smarter to spend 40 years doing something you enjoy (for peanuts) do not understand the time value of life. Do not waste time arguing with them: Smile, nod and agree
									- Follow the $$$ not your dreams. Why? Your dreams are what you *would* be doing if you had money!
								- 4) We have listed 10 life skills that everyone will need to use with the highest ROI:
									- Sales, public speaking, writing, reading, quick math, playing dumb, dating, health & fitness, technology and design.
								- 5) Once you find your skills. Once you have a solid understanding of the ten life skills. And. Once you have comfort in which skill you will be *good* at… Then you go niche:
									- e.g. Motivational Speaker
										- While we would never be caught dead at a motivational seminar after seeing them back in our teens… They are cash cows. People *will pay* a lot of money to “feel like a winner” for a short period of time. Of course it is all a sham. But. This is also why people get emotional over their home sports team. People will pay thousands of dollars to be associated with a winner if they cannot win themselves. (notice you’re combining many of the 10 skills here)
									- Private Gym Ownership
										- Becoming a physical trainer is generally a losing battle. You’re trading your time for money. Starting an actual gym can be outstanding. Private trainers make $100+ per hour and if your knowledge exceeds theirs… You can monetize your physical health knowledge to create a business. (notice you’re combining many of the 10 skills here)
									- Copywriting
										- In the case your main skill is writing and you have a solid understanding of sales… Learn to copywrite. If you become good enough… you can then charge a fee based on performance rather than an hourly rate. Even if your “dream” was playing in the NFL, you can go ancillary and sell NFL related items for a large margin. (notice you’re combining many of the 10 skills here)
						- **Identify Strengths**
						  collapsed:: true
							- AuthenticHappiness.org or StrengthsFinder for strengths test
							- How you choose to make money is dependent on several things
								- How much time do you have a week?
								- What resources do you have at your disposal?
								- Are you better at organization and managing talent/workers or are you able to work more efficiently predominantly self sufficient?
								- -
								- What is your background? What skills/network have you accrued?
								- Have you ever done anything related to digital marketing, site development, content creation, sales, etc. etc?
								- What state/country are you located in?
								- What are you interested in learning about and why?
						- **My Strengths**

							  e.g. interviewing, inperson networking, inperson pitching

							- Entrepreneurial Skills
							  id:: 644c0a7b-93c9-47de-80ef-18a9bca521fe
							  #BusinessCommitmentAnalysis-Skills
								- _Criteria_
									- Evergreen
									  Remains useful and valuable for a long-time
										- What % of knowledge learned will be useful over lifetime? How much over next 5 years?
										- e.g.
											- Creative - can't be easily automated by robots or AI
											- Skills accumulate e.g. web startups teach many marketing skills
											- Balance of skill and creativity requiremenet
											  #BusinessCommitmentAnalysis https://dynalist.io/d/mXC8LQ8sDMg7vNFI62mDSXJI#z=f5yDQDxDsF23f1T9b9JwZbPB
									- Barrier to Entry
										- e.g.
											- Requires high IQ
											- High trust - can't be outsourced to third world
									- notes
										- By choosing a legitimate career you are doing multiple things: 1) you become less replaceable – downside protection, 2) have numbers to point to in terms of revenue generation – ability to leave to a competitor, 3) be paid based on performance – generate significantly more in a macro up tick, 4) let your savings buy you precious time and….  the REAL point By going into a revenue generating role you are building skills that will be directly transferable to starting your own successful business in the future
										- If you can afford to get it outsourced, do. A professional oft times can do a better job than you can. Don’t waste your time stumbling through something, stick to what you are good at. This doesn’t mean don’t enrich your skills as over time it will make you a more competent manager but your 3rd grader drawing photoshop ebook cover may be enough but could cost you longer term.
								- Management
								  e.g. delegation, project management - especially agile or lean software dev method

								- Product creation
								  #ProductCreation https://workflowy.com/#/c2c4024dcfa0
									- Especially recurring income
										- Membership sites
								- Sales
								  eg marketing, networking, copywriting

									  #Sales https://workflowy.com/#/305a8cf11799

							- Choose the idea that can be tested the quickest and cheapest then execute immediately
							- First time entrepreneurs should avoid product creation especially

						- Minimum Viable Products to test Need (MVPs)
						  collapsed:: true
							- Businesses are hypotheses. You're testing to see if the market will buy.
							- ---
								- Do inexpensive (time and money-wise) tests before investing heavily.
								- Worry about the first dollar, first, not the first million.
								- Explain what the main goal of what you want your business to do.
								- Specific targets - Cheaper to market to.
								- "Totem pole theory" - where is your business on the proverbial totem pole?
								- You're more creative if you have less money to spend
							- 1) Determine Variables for Testing
								- E.g. Systematic Scale Offline Marketing

									- Consultation: Inperson/Phone
									- Prospect: General Manager/Owner
									- Close: Proposal first/Invoice immediately

									- Variations to test:
										- Location: Bandung+Bali
										- Target: Hotel Midrange+Hotel Budget+Restaurant Midrange+Villa Management
										- Lead Gen: Cold Call
										- Consultation: Inperson
										- Prospect: Owner ideally
										- Close: Proposal first
									- Offline: Potential Targets + Maps
										- Web Design - start with highest LTV per customer businesses eg lawyers, doctors, dentists, opticians,
										- Copywriting - Look for highest revenue generating businesses that rely on brand to differentiate eg hotels, lawyers, doctors, dentists,
										- ------------
										- ORM - Review sites like yelp and trip advisor. Hotels, restaurants, clubs
										- Systems Admin - businesses which heavily rely on online booking/web presence eg hotels, ad agency, real estate management,
										- Data Security - lawyers, hospitals,
										- Customer management - hospitals, spas, gyms,
										- Maps

							- Need vs Want
								- Two very different things. Systems & habits are what people need, but they want other things.
								- Want = what they actually pay for. Look if there's competition in your niche or are they currently spending money for solutions to same problem - otherwise you may be wasting time. Why aren't others doing it? Don't re-invent the wheel.

					- Steps
					  collapsed:: true
						- Pick your niche - again, something you can at least tolerate.
						- Look up keywords within that niche, see the difficulty and feasability for ranking within that area of expertise
						- Note the BUYER INTENT keywords and how saturated that market is - that is where there is real money to be made
						- Begin by writing - no need to build the site first. If you can't manage to write a few posts about the subject then it's probably not for you in the first place
						- So yes, this means you must find products that you are comfortable selling. Ideally, for your first site, it should be something you've actually used and has made your life better. A good rule of thumb is that if it's a digital product (courses, eBooks, etc), you should be
				- One niche, multiple income streams (that's how you should do diversification)

				- Market Viability
					- Market Size
					  id:: 644c0a7b-3c6d-4f2b-8d12-2d846b988299
					  collapsed:: true
					  #MarketSize
						- Overview - Authority vs Niche sites
							- Authority - Pros
							  collapsed:: true
								- Don't diversify with different markets
									- They are actually hurting their growth because they can’t leverage their expertise, product creation, customers, and time to grow faster by concentrating on one big market.
									- Victor Pride proposes multiple income streams on authority sites via each article as an income stream (affiliate/own products)
									  source:: https://boldanddetermined.com/start-a-blog-that-makes-money-forever/
										- Victor pride also uses satellite sites - niche sites which carry the authority of your main site but they give you the freedom to design them in other ways.
										- You want multiple sources of income but not multiple sources of focus.
								- Big Markets Are Awesome
									- You have a ton of people coming into these markets daily creating a never ending supply of customers, more possible partnerships (never consider people competition), and the ability to sell more than one product to each customer.
									- Selling something like how to get rid of acne will get you a sale but you have nothing to sell them afterwards. Now you look at something like dating, you have seen all the products that people are buying (text game, night game, day game, hipster game, celeb game, etc..)
								- Mike Cernovich has recommended putting all related topics on one site at start - only later deciding when and if to create niche sites
							- Niche - Pros
							  collapsed:: true
								- Authority sites aren't likely to target it because it's a fringe topic (low competition, thus quick to test viability)
								- Often these have more commercial interest
								- These are especially useful to target topics which may not be suitable for Habit Laboratory
								- Not expected to produce much content on blog/videos/ebook/newsletter (quick MVP)
						- Largest markets
							- Health -- Fitness, food, supplements, memory, anti-aging
							- Beauty - skin, fat loss, muscle gain
							- Money -- make money, close debts
							- Relationships -- get laid, find a man, revitalize your marriage
							- Expensive Hobbies -- golf, dogs, yachting, travel
						- Identifying Size of Market
							- Active communities
							  collapsed:: true
								- Active blogs
								- Active Facebook Pages
								- Active Forums
								- Active Email Newsletters
							- Google Keyword Tool
							  collapsed:: true
								- Average search volume (too high and better to look for a subsection, too low and it's not big enough)
								- 30,000+ Google searches per month on your top 3 keywords
							- Growth Trends
							  collapsed:: true
								- Ideally Evergreen (no seasonal change in demand)
									- with High Longevity (market size not rapidly declining)
								- Google Trends
									- https://www.google.com/trends/
								- Predict upcoming trends
									- Technology
									- Sports
									- Movies
					- People Willing to Pay to Fulfill This Desire
					  collapsed:: true
					  Choose a subset of larger market that would be best to target e.g. nerdfitness went for nerds within fitness niche
						- Validity Questions
						  collapsed:: true
							- 4 Validity Questions
								- Are people aware of this pain or desire?
								- Are people actively looking for a solution?
								- Can people afford to pay for a solution?
								- Are people willing to pay for a solution?
							- Best way to answer these 4: Are there already people selling products for this pain/desire?
								- Competition is a good thing e.g. dating bootcamps, IBM time management course,
								- Especially if they advertise
								- Find a profitable market and all you need to do is sell to them
									- A solid business niche, one that solves a legitimate pain point, just needs to be marketed
									- No time-consuming need to 'build an audience' - buy traffic to show them what solution you have
						- Willing to Spend money
						  id:: 644c0a7b-82fa-4930-a46f-7dad1e537201
						  collapsed:: true
						  #SpendthriftAudience
							- The Masses eg overweight people, emotionally-driven women
							  Give them validation. Give them attention. Make their life easier or less effortful eg one "quick fix"
							  
							  Pump them with emotion eg motivational stuff
							  
							  However: ""People make good money selling to the rich. But the rich got rich selling to the masses.""
							- Has to be an industry where millions-billions are being spent annually e.g. hospitality, alternative energy etc
							- It's easiest to reach certain markets e.g. right now I've got access to customer-facing businesses easily (offline marketing) so should focus on that
							- (more personal growth potential surrounding myself with smart individuals + higher earnings per client)
							- The 1%
							   (more personal growth potential surrounding myself with smart individuals + higher earnings per client)
								- Successful Businesses
								- The Wealthy
							- Those who want it the most, need it the least.
							   Eg fitness blogs seeking out fitness enthusiasts to easily sell products rather than trying to convince unhealthy people. Internet marketers selling make money online courses to wannabe internet marketers, rather than chasing salespeople or tech-savvy people
							  
							  Big difference between what people **want** and what they **need**
								- Understand what they will say; what they won't say (embarrassment etc); and what they can't say (evolutionary motivations, unconscious repressed etc)
								- > very subtlely acknowledge the unconscious motivations, usually when closing deal
								- See #Desire https://workflowy.com/#/af1ec707ac0d
							- Rich vs The Masses - the two markets
								- Law #1 – There Are Two Markets: There are two distinct markets. The masses and the rich. If you’re looking to make money you have to make a decision on which market you will cater to. The masses will focus on anything that does not require them to do any work and the rich will look for status symbols. This is overly simplistic but it’s a good starting point. Trying to target both markets is extremely difficult as they have business models that are largely unrelated (an exception may be the shiny new iPhone). Products that sell to the masses typically have *lower* margins with high volumes while products that sell to the rich have *high* margins with lower volumes.
								- Law #2 – Sell the Dream to the Masses: If you go down the masses route you’re going to sell them with the following tactics: 1) external motivation like Tony Robbins – “Unleash Your Inner Beast with This Product” or “Fire your Boss!”, 2) magic pills that will fix their life – “This solution has doctors hoping it will be banned!” (modafinil/provigil is one of the latest fads), 3) minimal work for sudden riches – “work from home and earn $100K tomorrow with no work!” and 4) celebrity endorsements – “XYZ celeb uses it!”. The theme here is that they are not responsible for their own actions and there is always some “hack” to getting what they want (without building any skills). When you’re selling to the masses do not put anything logical in the sales pitch. Keep it emotional and make sure your product works. Sure you can get away with bad products but that just leads to non-recurring income.
								- Law #3 – Sell the Prestige to the Rich: Get rich by selling them on the status of the item. The product can be largely the same but it needs to be *exclusive*. The best example is black and white balls and other such “filters” for rich people to go and meet. The cost is practically the same just sell those tickets for hundreds of dollars a piece! Another great example is the classic “bottle service” experience. No doubt about it that pricing alcohol 300% above its market value is going to be profitable. Notice, these can be done online, offer exclusive “packages” to people.
						- B2B generally pays more than B2C
						- Leftists also more likely to spend money as their lives are less fulfilling
						  source:: http://www.rooshv.com/why-progressive-media-is-the-path-to-riches
						- Women buy more than men, this is true in almost every single niche
						- Tech-savvy people are least likely to purchase (they know about torrent sites for example)
						  collapsed:: true
							- The more tech savvy someone is, the less likely they’ll buy something that’s available for free somewhere else. For ebooks, the biggest buying demographic is middle age women who aren’t particularly good with computers i.e. finding torrent sites
						- Nerds
						  collapsed:: true
							- Self-prescribed nerds have money.  They’re intelligent, they have jobs, they always want more information.  They’re probably a little socially awkward, so even more susceptible to the this-will-fix-you-nerd sales pitch
							- Worked well for nerdfitness.com
						- Fat people
						  collapsed:: true
							- Low willpower, impulsive
						- Feminists
						  collapsed:: true
							- Irrational
						- With disposable income

							- Direct competition (They are doing exactly what you want to do.  This is a good thing.)
							- Indirect competition (They are selling to the same market, but don’t sell what you do)
							- Affiliate offers (Check Clickbank, Share-A-Sale and Commission Junction for related affiliate offers)
							- Advertisers (Where there are well-known advertisers, there is money)
							- Gurus (Look for very influential people associated with your market)
							- eCommerce Sites (Find sites that are selling products to this market)

					- Pricing Strategy
					  id:: 644c0a7b-fea6-458e-93d3-33bcf1166ef0
						- Customer Perception
							- Do you want to be perceived as a high end business, if so, certain things like coupons, discounts, or promotions can't fit in your sales funnel. BUT that doesn't mean "added benefits" can't eg Apple's supreme customer service
						- Perceived value due to price
						- Compete on price or value (quality, perceived value, brand)
						- See https://workflowy.com/#/c9162dea01e5 sales letter
						- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pricing
						- Ending the price with "9"
							- e.g. £1.49 > £1.40
							- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psychological_pricing
							- However it expresses value, not premium
						-
						- Price comparison
						  intralink2:: https://workflowy.com/#/c9162dea01e5
						- _Principles_
						  collapsed:: true
							- Anchoring
							  collapsed:: true
								- Anchoring or focalism is a cognitive bias that describes the common human tendency to rely too heavily on the first piece of information offered (the "anchor") when making decisions. During decision making, anchoring occurs when individuals use an initial piece of information to make subsequent judgments. Once an anchor is set, other judgments are made by adjusting away from that anchor, and there is a bias toward interpreting other information around the anchor. For example, the initial price offered for a used car sets the standard for the rest of the negotiations, so that prices lower than the initial price seem more reasonable even if they are still higher than what the car is really worth.
								- Usage:
								  collapsed:: true
									- e.g. order of delivery options shown. Choose the best value option for **us** as the first one, as it acts like a psychological _default_ choice for them
							- Bracketing
							  collapsed:: true
								- Paraphrased - more overall profit when using 3 price points (rather than 2) and if the 3rd option is a premium cost
								  [archived] https://fizzle.co/sparkline/most-common-pricing-mistake
								- According to William Poundstone, author of "Priceless: The Myth of Fair Value (and How to Take Advantage of It)," in a New York Magazine interview, "The main role of that $115 platter — the only three-digit thing on the menu — is to make everything else near it look like a relative bargain."
								- Instead of comparing prices of your products to your competitors', they instead only compare within your product selection - and choose which is the best value out of all your products
								- Restaurants often offer foods in two portion sizes. This strategy is called bracketing. The customer has no idea how much smaller the small portion is, so they assume it's the best value price because it costs less. What they don't realize is that the restaurant wanted to sell the smaller portion at the lower price all along, and simply used the bigger portion with the higher price as comparison.
								- There's a phenomenon in advertising called "Decoy Pricing".
								  collapsed:: true
									- When two objects (cars, liquor, computers, doesn't matter) have similar specifications, but one is slightly more expensive than the other - it becomes difficult to justify spending the extra money. (You can then take the average amount of money spent, per purchase.)
									- Decoy pricing involves placing a similar third object, next to the two original objects, whose price is extremely expensive. No one buys the highest price object, BUT the presence of the third object makes it easier to rationalize buying the slightly higher-priced object. (Indeed, the average price per purchase significantly increases in all cases of Decoy Pricing.)
									- So I view Richard Spencer as a decoy-priced item. Without Spencer, the choice is between Paul Ryan and Trump. But with Spencer around, the choice is between Paul Ryan, Trump, and Spencer. Decoy pricing says this second arrangement benefits Trump.
							- Give a free delivery option
							  collapsed:: true
								- People love *free*
								  source:: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Predictably_Irrational#The_Cost_of_Zero_Cost
								- It's a loss leader
								  collapsed:: true
									- A loss leader is a product that has a price set below the operating margin. Loss leadering is widely used in supermarkets and budget-priced retail outlets where the store as a means of generating store traffic. The low price is widely promoted and the store is prepared to take a small loss on an individual item, with an expectation that it will recoup that loss when customers purchase other higher priced-higher margin items. In service industries, loss leadering may refer to the practice of charging a reduced price on the first order as an inducement and with anticipation of charging higher prices on subsequent orders.

								  collapsed:: true
									- Free delivery option (despite it being low quality delivery tracking, speed and packaging)

							- Yeah I think for a graphic design team you'll have to deal with lots of "can you change this, add that, make it more in the style of..." That I don't have to deal with, so it totally makes sense for you guys to be hourly / daily rate.
							- We regularly get asked for fixed price though. Thankfully I can normally produce a file from the same client called 'Final_v9' to illustrate why we don't give them the fixed price they want.

							  collapsed:: true
								- 1
									- What the heck subscription models are you using? In my experience the software gets broken over time and you can't do anything about it, including RUN THE OLD VERSION WHICH WORKED. There's no longer as much incentive for companies to improve because they'll get paid anyway instead of payment being contingent on actually delivering something useful. Yes subscription models are good for developers, no this doesn't make the software better, it makes it worse. (or at the very least to improve much slower)
									- I basically pirate everything that requires a subscription now or us a FOSS alternative, even though there was a period of time where I would pay for software between when I was poor and had to pirate everything and now when I can afford to pay for subscriptions but the situation with lack of control over SaaS is so fucking untenable that I just can't. I don't believe SaaS is moral, it basically removes all the power from the consumer and allows the software providers to rake you over the coals over and over. I'm not interested in funding such things. I feel more comfortable with piracy than I do with SaaS on a philosophical level.
									- SaaS companies don't innovate, they buy competitors because they're using an abusive business model with bad incentives.
									- P.S. SaaS _can_ probably be implemented in a decent way, and some SaaS does actually provide an ongoing service, but in general I believe my point stands.
						- Price matching
							- Great way for bigger businesses to beat out newcomer businesses via attrition
							  collapsed:: true
								- The utterly inefficient and overpriced Canadian telecoms exist only because we allow one specific behavior:
								- 1.A new local player comes to town, builds their own infrastructure (e.g. connects one building to fiber) and starts offering competitive 
								  service.
								- 2. Rogers/Shaw/Bell/Telus immediately offer better terms for the residents of that building.
								- 3. The competitor runs out of money and leaves.
								- 4. The telecoms revert to their usual pricing.

				- Ideal Audience
				  collapsed:: true
					- My avatar (Customer Profile)
						- So the first, most important question is, "who do we want to change?"
						- If you can't answer this specifically, do not proceed to the rest. By who, I mean, "give me a name." Or, if you can't give me a name, then a persona, a tribe, a spot in the hierarchy, a set of people who share particular worldviews. People outside this group should think you're crazy, or at the very least, ignore you.
						- Then, be really clear about:
						  collapsed:: true
							- What does he already believe?
							- What is he afraid of?
							- What does he think he wants?
							- What does he actually want?
							- What stories have resonated with him in the past?
							- Who does he follow and emulate and look up to?
							- What is his relationship with money?
							- What channel has his permission? Where do messages that resonate with him come from? Who do they trust and who does they pay attention to?
							- What is the source of their urgency need for your solution? Why would they change now rather than later?
							- After he has changed, what will he tell his friends?
						- Now that you know these things, go make a product and a service and a story that works. No fair changing the answers to the questions to match the thing you've already made (you can change the desired audience, but you can't change the truth of what they want and believe).
						- Avatar
						  collapsed:: true
							- Demo/psychographic
								- 18-40 year old males, who value open-mindedness, controversial non-PC viewpoints, questioning authority, courage and lifestyle design.
							- Pains
								- soul crushing 9-5, getting rejected by women, being unhealthy, poor productivity, negative friends/family
							- Desires
								- Attracting feminine women, being more courageous, starting a business, travelling the world, building muscle, finding purpose/meaning, gaining virtues e.g. discipline
					- story/background of the ideal customers life, their hobbies, interests, age range, education, etc
					- psychographics, demographics
					- You can always provide value to someone
						- There may be Level 10s in **X **topic (e.g. Fitness) and you're only a Level 5. This means however that beginners can relate better to you, you don't have the curse of expert knowledge (which can harm teaching ability), people may just prefer your delivery and character too
				- Get Audience Feedback Frequently
				  id:: 644c0a7b-de9d-4a2d-9cf7-8ef9e703cded
				  collapsed:: true

					- Surveys
						- Survey Post
							- Ask your audience what kind of content they'd like you to make
							- e.g. http://www.dangerandplay.com/2014/07/11/reader-survey/ embedded Ninja Form
							  I am considering some radical changes to Danger & Play and need your help.
							  
							  We have the same vision. I want Danger & Play to improve, and so do you.
							  
							  Taking this brief survey will help me give you more of what you want.
							  
							  **Danger & Play Reader Survey**
							  Where did you hear about Danger & Play?
							  What other websites or blogs do you read?
							  Do you listen to the podcasts?
							  What do you want to see more of?
							  What is one improvement I could make to Danger & Play?
							  Have you ever taken a nootropic before?
							  What benefit do you look for in a nootropic?
							  How old are you?
							- http://www.dangerandplay.com/2014/06/28/who-are-you/
							- http://www.dangerandplay.com/2014/07/15/can-answer-reader-question/
						- You can also contact your best fans on Twitter directly with a direct message or @reply. Ask them to give you feedback through the survey.
						- Reach out to anyone in your personal network who fits the description of your target audience. Ask them to take the survey as well.
						- Look for an opportunity to talk to at least one person in person or over the phone or through Skype. One-on-one conversations provide incredibly valuable insight.

							- Take personal responsibility for their failures, but not their success
							- People work harder for recognition and achievement than anything else
								- So highlight their achievements regularly e.g. monthly habit reviews
						- Treat current fans better than new fans

					- Customer Service recording issues
						- Analyse and categorise issues
						- it's very very difficult to gauge good customer service, since customers aren't complaining. They rarely go and write great reviews. BUT bad customer service is easy to spot. A pissed off individual will always write a bad review, and go out of their way to talk shit about the business and not recommend it to their friends/family/associates.
						- The key I see to gauging great customer service is in the follow up. Once a person buys something for you, follow up a week later, whether it's a phone call, an automated email, or something that causes/sparks a conversation.
							- See if you can get a review, a referral, a repeat purchase
					- Product feedback
						- In general, my basic rule is that if it distracts from my writing, I avoid it eg reading reviews. This also includes obsessing over my stats all day long. I monitor them daily. Some people do more or less but that is just what works well for me without disrupting my work flow too much.
						- In the end, I only care about two things once I publish a title - sales and returns. I have targets for both. In my mind, this is the market voting for my work and so they are the only metrics I care about. What people say about my work doesn't matter. All I care about is what their wallet/purse says about it.
						- Though useful insights can be gleamed from some reviews
					- Comments & Emails
						- Emails
							- Organize my email box. Ensure that every email (that is not parasitic or asking for something for nothing or otherwise is stupid and time wasting) gets a response from me.
						- Comments
							- Promote my coaching or products via comment replies
								- E.g. Self Hacked comments
								  1) The studies show RS as the single most effective tool for weightloss for people who are overweight. If you are underweight it won’t make you more underweight.
								  
								  2) I had very similar issues. Your daughter probably also has insulin resistance, OCD, attentional issues and other cognitive problems. Her inability to exercise is due to mitochondrial breakdown. Cancer, heart disease, neurodegenerative diseases and/or cognitive decline will ensue. The good news is these issues can be fixed, but they need to be tackled from multiple angles. Much better sooner than later. You can see what you can glean from the blog or email me about a consult. Upto you.
								  
								  Best,
								  Joe
							- Haters are a good litmus test of whether you're on the right path. Its part and parcel of the journey.
								- With every person I first debate > if they don't listen to logic then ignore > then only if they're disrupting the experience of your readers by trolling many tweets would I then block.
							- See Blog Comments - on Platform for pros and cons
						- Roosh: The snobby author is dead. While I can’t reply to every tweet or comment left on my blog, I do respond to over 90% of my emails, even though it takes a considerable amount of time. Today’s writer should be more like a friend than an aloof celebrity.
							- There's pro/cons vs reply more in public vs in private. Be seen as human by many, vs degrading value. Replying to emails which is unexpected vs only benefitting one person at a time
					- Minimum Viable Audience
						- enough feedback from comments, email, social media in order to solve their problems
						- The audience will tell you, unsolicited, about problems and desires that no existing product solves. They’ll tell you      how existing products and services are missing the boat, or how the companies behind them are less than desirable.
						- + create a network of loyal fans/friends who will test idea
							- Biggest risk as an entrepreneur is building something no-one actually wants
						- serve audience to find new ways to deliver value

						- HINT: look what free value your audience is enjoying e.g. email content, which posts etc

		- Choose Brand Name
		  collapsed:: true
			- Domain Name
				- You main keyword phrase - Having your main keyword phrase in your domain name will help with your search rankings. For me, it’s ideal if someone can read your domain name and at a glance “get” what your site is all about.
				- Positional statement - In the same way, a positional adjective can help people understand what they are getting before they visit your site. For example words like help, advice, tips, etc.
			- Eli Altman's "Don't Call It That": http://amzn.to/2wCyEcnIt is easily the best $10 kindle book I've come across for naming strategies, picking solid names and understanding the flaws in poor name selection.His company also has a fantastic blog where they give away a fair bit of their secret sauce for free: http://www.ahundredmonkeys.com/category/all-posts/
