			  collapsed:: true
				- _Lead Generation_
					- Finding gigs
						- Where to get clients
							- Have your own website you can point clients to
							- _Online businesses:_
								- _Low barrier of entry/low rate?:_
									- Freelance portals
										- Elance, Odesk, Guru.com, Freelancer.com, Staff.com, Craigslist, Fiverr (Matt Forney book), http://entrylevelwriter.com/
										- May need to write for content pay sites to get some credibility to leverage
										- Elance Strategy
											- Searching for Elance gigs
												- Specific fixed-price writing gigs for U.S. clients only http://bit.ly/1EexBKZ
												- Remember: 40% of Elance jobs are never awarded. A 10% acceptance rate is good. A 5% acceptance rate is totally acceptable.
												- BID FOR TOMORROW, NOT FOR TODAY.
													- keep searching for jobs and bidding on work when you’re tied up with paying projects
												- 19. LOOK FOR FIRST-TIME BUYERS + WEB DESIGN COMPANIES (they need a copy guy)
													- buyers who have a longer history on Elance have become used to receiving incredibly low bids for their jobs.
												- 21. SUCCESS ATTRACTS SUCCESS.
													- Look for buyers requiring writing services for an already-successful, sustainable venture.
													- Do your homework on the buyer. - username + location
													- Stay away from buyers who “have a great story to tell about their amazing lives”.
													- Avoid buyers whose business model relies on some kind of get-rich-quick Internet business.
													- Stay away from buyers who post poorly written job descriptions that are short on details.
												- Look for prospects in my area of expertise - psychology, marketing, health, nutrition
												- Warning Signs - “This is an easy job if you know what you’re doing.” / “This job is perfect for a college student or anyone who wants to earn a little extra money.” / “I would do this myself but I’m just too busy.” / “This shouldn’t take you more than a few hours.”
											- Proposal Copy
												- Or video proposal
													- 1-3m enthusiastic video pitch from some exotic locale
													- Wear a shirt
													- Ideally bid on similar jobs to keep re-using the vid
												- Start
													- Credibility Section
														- **Opening sentence - frame what they need**
														- “A truly effective sales letter needs to be written by a writer with boots-on- the-ground sales experience. I’m a copywriter with a proven track record of writing words that sell, including copy for....
														- OR: “For this project, you’ll need a writer with proven creativity. My accolades as a writer include award-winning journalism articles....
														- From my experience as a Behaviour Analyst and Dating Coach, I have a strong understanding of the underlying motivations which compel us towards action. I've taken these insights into numerous other ventures including a personal blog and ad buying campaigns for a pharmaceuticals company.
														- If I'm honest I have little experience with hearing aids specifically. But I am eager to learn all about the fears, desires and complete psychological profile of your target audience.
														- **Credible Value Points**
														- ------
														- ********************************
														- **Tested TOP 5%-20% in Elance tests**
														- I've trained as a marketing and persuasion expert for the past 6 years with such achievements as:
														- Copywriting, media buying and conversion optimisation for the acclaimed men's sexual health pharmaceutical Andro-Plus
														- Being the top Dating Coach of the internationally renowned love coaching company Maaximum Seduction
														- Working as a Behaviour Analyst for 3 years with a wide variety of clients such as children with autism or juvenile delinquents
														- ********************************
														- -------
														- Native English speaker - Born and raised in London, I have a superb command of the English language.
														- Individual - All work is done by me and never delegated or outsourced.
														- I'm in a different time zone to you - this means you’re able to “work through the night” and deliver overnight service on rush jobs.
													- Attach 2-4 *relevant* PDF samples AND link to portfolio
													- Copycat the buyer's language and proposal
												- End:
													- Ending - you’re available to answer their questions or discuss any aspect of the project further, so if there are any details they want to resolve, they can simply ask you directly.
													- If you have any questions or wish to discuss any aspect of the project further you can ask me directly (contact details will be shared upon acceptanc
													- Regards, [fullname]
												- Final Checks:
													- 200-400 word proposals, go for volume > perfection
													- You have to deliver or risk bad feedback/losing gig - (be REALISTIC when working out time estimates, and then add another 15%!)
													- DON’T BAD-MOUTH OTHER CONTRACTORS
														- it’s never in your best interests to draw the buyer’s attention to the flaws of other providers. It shows that you don’t trust them to make the right choice without your guidance, comes off as controlling, and makes you look petty and negative:
														- NOT THIS: “Unlike some of the other bidders, I’m not some amateur looking to earn some extra pocket money. I’m a full-time, professional writer who takes her profession very seriously.”
														- OR THIS: “Don’t put your project in the hands of one of these writing companies from India. They are known for delivering substandard, barely legible copy. Trust your project to a qualified, native English speaker like me.”
														- BUT THIS: “Your company’s tagline is its touchstone, and you need a marketing-savvy copywriter who knows how to get to the core of your company’s values and aspirations. With a certificate in marketing, five years of copywriting experience, and several great taglines in my portfolio, I have the proven expertise to deliver a tagline that will truly..."
													- Don't bad mouth the buyer + compliment them
														- BUT THIS: “Thank you for providing a link to your current website; it helped me understand your business and your objectives better. There is a lot of great product information contained in the site, and I would love to see it organized in a way that made it easier for your potential customers to find what they needed. If selected for the project, I would begin by...”
														- A little courtesy and flattery can go a long way
														- Complimenting their business or some aspect of their existing writing materials sets a positive tone and makes the buyer feel good about the project and the role you have to play in it.
														- FOR EXAMPLE: “I reviewed the brochure attached to your job description, and I really like the tagline you chose for your business. I think it does a great job of summing up the benefits you bring your clients, and I would want to build on that tagline when rewriting the brochure...”
														- OR: “Thanks for providing a link to your website. I spent some time looking at it, and I’m intrigued by your software product! What a great idea— I had no idea that this kind of technology was so affordable. You’ve done an excellent job of explaining how it works on the website, and I think the case studies you need written will really help to convey the benefits of TotalSoft to small business customers. As a writer with five years of experience writing for the software industry, I can...”
											- Example proposal
												- 1 - some ebook author
													- **********************************
													- **Tested TOP 5% - 10% in eLance tests**
													- I've been writing high converting, high ranking copy for the last 5 years. My clients have included:
													-  Trainers in Anthony Robbins's organization.
													-  Bryan Franklin, sales trainer and executive coach to Fortune 500 companies like Apple, Google,
													- Cisco and Logitech.
													-  A Washington real estate broker whose firm has handled over $120 million in real estate sales
													- since 2006 and scored in the top 1% in sales volume in 2011.
													-  Demand Media, a NYSE listed company currently valued at over $500 million dollars.
													-  Plus many others. I've written well over 3,000 high quality articles throughout my career.
													- Delivery time is 72 hours.
													- **********************************
												- myself
													- Relevant Experience and Qualifications:
													- I'm glad you have high aspirations for your brand. Too many set their expectations low and flood the market with yet another crappy product which leaves their customers unsatisfied so sells poorly, which is a waste of time for everyone involved.
													- To help achieve your goals you need 2 things: an expert in 1) Marketing and Persuasion 2) Sports Nutrition.
													- 1)
													- I've been a marketing and persuasion expert for the past 6 years with such achievements as:
													- Copywriting, media buying and conversion optimisation for the acclaimed men's health and fitness drug Andro-Plus
													- Being a top Dating Coach of the internationally renowned love coaching company Maaximum Seduction
													- Working as a Behaviour Analyst for 3 years with a wide variety of clients such as children with autism or juvenile delinquents
													- 2)
													- I've tried hundreds of health supplements including NAC, BCAAs, whey protein, choline, liver caps, PQQ, ZMA, melatonin and lots more.
													- I'm an avid bodybuilder also and have tried almost everything like carb cycling, low carb diet, intermittent fasting, low level laser therapy, cryotherapy, high intensity training, etc.
													- I deeply understand the audience involved as I am part of that audience who regularly uses supplements for improved performance and physique.
													- Attached are two product reviews completed recently.
													- Approach to the Job:
													- Cost per words: $1
													- Cost for 1250 word rewrite: $1250
													- If you have any questions feel free to ask.
													- Regards,
													- Aaron Sollesse
												- me x2
													- Later can highlight Elance feedback + portfolio + repeat business > customised bids with creative solutions
													- "My x% positive Elance feedback attests to the quality of my work, my respect for deadlines and my commitment to meeting the needs of my clients."
													- "For insight into the quality of the work I produce, please browse my excellent Elance client feedback."
													- "For insight into the quality of the work I deliver, please take a moment to review my Elance client feedback, and note my exceptionally high rate of repeat business (48%)."
													- End with:
													- Regards, Aaron Sollesse
													- Intro: customised credibility introduction + links to relevant samples
													- "I'm a copywriter with a background in marketing communications and expertise in writing for the software/technology industry. By using clear language and benefit-based copy, I make compelx products easy for the general public to understand and appreciate."
													- "At $65, my hourly rate is higher than the range you specified, but I believe you need a senior copywriter to do justice to your brand and protect your unique and evolving voice. I think you'll also find that hiring a more experienced writer will save you time and money in the long run - more effective copy, better results, and less hand-holding."
													- "At $65, my hourly rate is higher than your estimate, but I think you'll find value in the services of a skilled, senior writer who's familiar with your business model."
													- "My bid for $2,500 includes:
													- All client liaison, interviewing, and information-gathering
													- Delivery of eight pages of web content (250 words per)
													- Delivery of two case studies (800 words per)
													- Up to six corporate bios (250 words per)
													- Two rounds of edits to original drafts as needed
													- Final review and proofing of content in-template"
												- 3
													- I'm perfect for you.
													- From my experience as a Behaviour Analyst and Dating Coach, I have a strong understanding of the underlying motivations which compel us towards action. I've taken these insights into numerous other ventures including a personal blog and ad buying campaigns for a pharmaceuticals company.
													- If I'm honest I have little experience with hearing aids specifically. But I am eager to learn all about the fears, desires and complete psychological profile of your target audience.
													- ---
													- I know exactly the type of info marketing you're looking for. For this first project you'll receive from me a succinct yet comprehensive copy detailing all how hearing quality changes over life and the reasons why.
													- Peppered throughout the copy will be every benefit and pain point as to why they should get their hearing tested by your clinic, right now. The entire ad will be a soft sell for your clinic.
													- We can discuss specifics like dimensions of the leaflet, location, reach etc. when I've been accepted for this project in order to maximise ROI for your marketing campaign.
													- **********************************
													- **Tested TOP 5% - 10% in eLance tests**
													- I've been writing high converting, high ranking copy for the last 5 years. My clients have included:
													-  Trainers in Anthony Robbins's organization.
													-  Bryan Franklin, sales trainer and executive coach to Fortune 500 companies like Apple, Google,
													- Cisco and Logitech.
													-  A Washington real estate broker whose firm has handled over $120 million in real estate sales
													- since 2006 and scored in the top 1% in sales volume in 2011.
													-  Demand Media, a NYSE listed company currently valued at over $500 million dollars.
													-  Plus many others. I've written well over 3,000 high quality articles throughout my career.
													- Delivery time is 72 hours.
													- **********************************
													- Spend some time brainstorming your most compelling points and put together a concise yet compelling
													- snippet that you can use in the body of your proposals.
													- Again, I chose to highlight my writing experience because it’s my strongest point. However, if you don’t
													- have writing experience, don’t despair. I didn’t either when I first started. I just highlighted other traits,
													- such as having studied marketing and previous jobs I’d worked, to make my services look as presentable
													- as possible.
														- Each proposal you send should be customized with a short paragraph or two at the beginning. Read the job description carefully once or twice to get a sense of where the client is coming from and what they’re looking for. Then, write a paragraph or two that’s tailored just for the client. Describe what unique experience you have that’ll allow you to do a good job for that client. Write something that lets them know that you read their post and aren’t just “spraying” proposals at random. Include your template in the body of your proposal, then finish with the exact terms and conditions. For example, if you’re applying to write 20 articles for a health and fitness website, you might start off by talking about any experience you have in that field. Then you’ll paste in your template, then give details on your delivery time and pricing.
											- Bids + Work Expectations
												- Info
													- I know exactly the type of info marketing you're looking for. For this first project you'll receive from me a succinct yet comprehensive copy detailing all how hearing quality changes over life and the reasons why.
													- Peppered throughout the copy will be every benefit and pain point as to why they should get their hearing tested by your clinic, right now. The entire ad will be a soft sell for your clinic.
													- We can discuss specifics like dimensions of the leaflet, location, reach etc. when I've been accepted for this project in order to maximise ROI for your marketing campaign.
													- Choose which of the 3 price-breakdowns to use (test):
													- Number of articles:
													- Average word count:
													- Price per article:  (usually for big spenders)
													- Price per word: $0.03
													- Price per 100 words: $3
													- Total cost:
													- ALWAYS INCLUDE A BID AMOUNT WITH YOUR PROPOSAL
													- if the job descriptions states: “I need a website,” but doesn’t specify the number of pages, I will write: “Based on the assumption that your website will require eight pages of content (300 words per page), I am submitting a bid of $ 500. My bid can be adjusted to reflect a smaller or bigger website as per your requirements.” then I’ll urge them to contact me so that we can discuss the project in greater detail.
													- Terms and Conditions / Delivery Time and Pricing
													- Delivery time is 72 hours / x days.
												- >  only bid small jobs at first so as not to lock yourself in for a lot of work at cheap rates
												- Why to Bid Fixed-Price
													- I've changed it to $25 per hour. I was thinking of putting $5 per 100 words
													- Fixed price is where the money's at. Don't let bidding this type of work intimidate you. I can make $100 an hour on jobs I bid, but it's hard to ask $100 an hour from a client without them shitting their pants. On hourly jobs, I usually say $40 - $60. $30 is the lowest I'll go if I really need the work.
													- Think about it this way, if you go hourly, you have no variables you can introduce to up your rate besides asking for more money. If you learn to bid fixed jobs, you can
													- If you've got 5 reviews already, I'd put $20 - $30 an hour on your profile, just for branding purposes, and only bid on fixed jobs. If you bid on hourly, try to talk the client into switching to fixed bid. Present your bids by coming up with a per word or per 100 words rate.
													- Per job, but I usually break it down to them as a per word or per article price. The higher-end clients usually just want a per article price. Most clients order in batches. Don't ever do hourly. I've tried it, but most clients seem uncomfortable with how much I'm making per hour.
												- Don't be afraid to Bid High if you can show them expertise
													- However, you need to explain to the buyer why the job is worth more to them than they had originally anticipated. The key to doing this successfully is to focus on the value that the buyer will see in hiring a seasoned professional:
													- “As a writer with excellent credentials and five years of experience, I am used to earning upwards of $ 45 per hour for my website content. If you want a writer with my level of expertise, I’m afraid you will need to adjust your budget upwards. As a result, I am submitting a bid that is considerably higher than your stated budget range.”
													- OR: “Your website content is your calling card to the world, and can directly impact your professional image and your ability to win new business. The right web copy is an investment in your business: an investment that can pay off a hundredfold. As a writer with five years of experience and a proven track record of writing content that performs, I can deliver web copy that will convey the kind of confidence and professionalism your company needs to project. Because I believe your project requires the services of a more senior writer to do it justice, I am submitting a bid of $ 750 for the work...”
													- "At $65, my hourly rate is higher than the range you specified, but I believe you need a senior copywriter to do justice to your brand and protect your unique and evolving voice. I think you'll also find that hiring a more experienced writer will save you time and money in the long run - more effective copy, better results, and less hand-holding."
													- "At $65, my hourly rate is higher than your estimate, but I think you'll find value in the services of a skilled, senior writer who's familiar with your business model."
													- "My bid for $2,500 includes:
														- All client liaison, interviewing, and information-gathering
														- Delivery of eight pages of web content (250 words per)
														- Delivery of two case studies (800 words per)
														- Up to six corporate bios (250 words per)
														- Two rounds of edits to original drafts as needed
														- Final review and proofing of content in-template"
													- EXTRA TIP: Here’s a great way to hold on to your value, and still meet the price- conscious buyer halfway. Suggest to them that while you can’t deliver that exact project for a reduced fee, you can provide them with a slightly modified version. For instance, if they have asked for a 20-page report to be offered as part of an online squeeze campaign, tell them that you can deliver a more concise 12-page report within their budget, and suggest to them that readers tend to prefer shorter formats on the web. If you can find a creative solution that fits their budget and meets your own earning needs, you both come out winners.
												- Example price info
													- David Gor:
													- Informational articles of at least 500 words each about HCG.
													- I can do this @$0.04/word ($20/500 words). I can rewrite an article I've written (will pass as 100% unique) for $0.03/word.
													- Easier, more general articles e.g. on testosterone and men's health I can do at a slightly lower rate again.
											- Tips
												- I bid on a job to write 365 “love tips” for an iPhone app, and foolishly underestimated the time it would take. I wound up having to charge $ 200 for a weeks’ worth of work, because once you’ve promised something for a certain price on Elance, you have to deliver, or risk a) not getting paid or b) receiving terrible feedback that could scupper your Elance career. Lesson learned: be REALISTIC when working out time estimates, and then add another 15%!
												- 24. LEAD WITH YOUR MOST RELEVANT CREDENTIALS.
												- “Who are you, and what can you do for me?” Open each bid by telling the buyer why you’re the right person to hire.
												- “A truly effective sales letter needs to be written by a writer with boots-on- the-ground sales experience. I’m a copywriter with a proven track record of writing words that sell, including copy for a door-to-door flyer that cost $ 1,000 to distribute and resulted in about $ 10,000 in new business for Gino’s Pizza...”
												- OR: “For this project, you’ll need a writer with proven creativity. My accolades as a writer include award-winning journalism articles for The Canyon Tribute newspaper, and the publication of Bitsy Has An Adventure, a children’s book that has sold 10,000 copies...”
												- 25. INCLUDE YOUR MOST RELEVANT PORTFOLIO SAMPLES.
												- Don’t choose the ones you’re proudest of; choose the ones that are closest to the type and style of work the buyer is asking for.
												- If they’re looking for someone to write an ebook about managing diabetes, they are very likely to pick the contractor who can provide a writing sample on the topic of diabetes, or at the very least for a chronic health-related topic.
												- EXTRA TIP: Don’t refer buyers to your portfolio. Take the time to provide links or attachments to a few hand-picked, relevant samples.
												- Two to four attached or linked samples are all you need— you don’t want to overwhelm them! For longer works (reports, books and ebooks), I usually provide a two- to three-page sample, and let them know that the complete work is available upon request.
												- 26. GET THE EARLY-BIRD ADVANTAGE.
												- Being proactive about searching for work and bidding promptly can boost your acceptance rate in two ways: 1) it means your bid will be there if the buyer decides to award the job early and 2) it places your bid higher on the buyer’s proposal list, which means the buyer sees it first. (NOTE: Another way to rise to the top of the list of proposals is to place a “sponsored” bid. Look for the “Place mine at the top of the list” checkbox option when you submit your proposal, and be prepared to pay double the number of Connects for the privilege of having your bid come first or second in the list.)
												- 27. PRICE YOUR SERVICES COMPETITIVELY.
												- Take into account your education.
												- If you have a relevant degree, certificate or other qualification, this enhances your marketability, and should be reflected in your rates.
												- Take into account your years of relevant on-the-job experience.
												- Take into account any industry-specific or niche skills or experience.
												- If you are both a writer and a certified computer technician, and you are bidding on jobs requiring both writing skills and technological know-how, you should be charging a premium for your services.
												- Improve your chances by placing lower bids.
												- 28. USE YOUR “WATCH LIST” TO ANALYZE WINNING BIDS.
												- You can click the “Add to Watch List” button on any job description, and that job will be added to your Watch List. The Watch List will allow you to keep track of the number of bids each job has attracted, the high, low and average amount people have bid, and the status of the job (open, closed, or awarded).
												- You can use the Watch List to keep an eye on jobs you haven’t bid on, and this can give you a wealth of information you can use to improve your earnings and results on Elance. For instance, if there’s a new writing area you’re interested in, start watch-listing jobs in this area so that you can see how many are awarded, who wins them and what they charge for their services. This will tell you whether it’s a financially worthwhile area to explore. And by examining the profiles of the winning bidders, you’ll get a sense of the credentials needed to win the work.
												- 29. BE REALISTIC ABOUT YOUR SUCCESS RATE.
												- around 40% of Elance jobs are never awarded.
												- Yet my acceptance rate is 25%. That means for every 20 proposals I send out, five result in work. Does that help put it in perspective? A 10% acceptance rate is good. A 5% acceptance rate is totally acceptable.
												- 30. LEVERAGE YOUR ELANCE CREDIBILITY IN EVERY BID.
												- Here are some ways to leverage feedback in your proposals:
												- Mention your feedback score.
												- -Your overall feedback rating is an important element in your marketability. Although clients can see your rating clearly when you bid, it’s not a bad idea to draw attention to it to reinforce its value as an indicator of your quality: “As you will see from my 4.8 rating, my clients are highly satisfied with the quality of work I deliver...”
												- Quote your best feedback. Other clients’ feedback is an incredibly powerful selling point. Include a stellar quote in your bid whenever possible. Note: If you have feedback from a client in a similar industry as the prospective buyer, or for whom you delivered a similar product, use that specific feedback in your bid. For instance: “As an automotive manufacturer, you can be assured that I understand your needs. High-Speed Auto Co., one of my Elance clients, recently left this feedback on my profile page: ‘Hayden delivered great work. Highly recommended!’”
												- Mention your repeat business ratio.
												- “As you will see from my Elance profile, 15% of my clients love my work so much, they have hired me more than once.”
												- 31. LEARN FROM YOUR UNSUCCESSFUL BIDS.
												- take a look at the profile of the contractor who won the job. What do they have that’s better than you? More work experience? More completed Elance projects? More Elance dollars earned? A better-looking portfolio? Make a note of anything you admire about their profile and use the insights to constantly update and refine your own.
												- If you have had a chance to communicate with the prospective buyer on the Elance Private Message Board or by email, it can be very helpful to ask (very politely and respectfully) why they chose another contractor instead of you. This takes a delicate touch: you don’t want to sound upset or accusing. Try framing it this way: “thank you for the opportunity to bid on your job. I’m disappointed that I was not selected, but I wish you great success with the project. May I ask what made you decide to choose a different contractor’s services? Any input you can provide will help me improve my competitive advantage and my chances of winning future work with great clients like yourself.” If you are lucky enough to get a response, the insights you receive will be invaluable in helping you understand where you can improve your bidding approach and your skills.
												- 33. ALWAYS INCLUDE A BID AMOUNT WITH YOUR PROPOSAL.
												- checking off the “Submit amount later” box, so that they can submit a bid after getting additional project details from the buyer.
												- I find that I often need further clarification before being able to place my bid.
												- For instance, if the job descriptions states: “I need a website,” but doesn’t specify the number of pages, I will write: “Based on the assumption that your website will require eight pages of content (300 words per page), I am submitting a bid of $ 500. My bid can be adjusted to reflect a smaller or bigger website as per your requirements.” then I’ll urge them to contact me so that we can discuss the project in greater detail.
												- 34. SHOW BUYERS THAT YOU’RE TOTALLY ACCESSIBLE.
												- mentioning that you are available to discuss the project on Skype or other VoIP channel
												- And once a potential buyer responds to your proposal, you are free to provide them with any means of contact you want. Once a buyer has responded to me on Elance, I always sign off on any further communication with my URL, my email address, my local phone number and my Skype ID. This a) allows them to view more information and work samples on my website if they choose and b) shows them that I am always reachable through a range of communication channels.
												- 35. MARKET YOUR UNIQUE ELANCE ADVANTAGES.
												- If you are in the same time zone as the buyer, mention this, and point out the fact that you keep the same office hours and will be able to respond to their communications in a timely manner.
												- If you are in a different time zone, point out that this means you’re able to “work through the night” and deliver overnight service on rush jobs.
												- If you are a native English speaker, mention this fact to the buyer. You will be competing against many non-native English speakers, and your facility with the language will count in your favor.
												- If you are an individual, you can mention that all work is done by you and never delegated or outsourced.
												- 36. NEVER TRY TO WIN THE SYMPATHY VOTE.
												- “I am just starting out in my writing career, but I am willing to work hard and learn fast.” Or “Although I am new to freelance writing, I am an accomplished writer with several years of experience on the staff of a major newspaper.”
												- 37. USE COPYCAT TECHNIQUES TO MATCH THE BUYER’S STYLE.
												- e.g. be casual if they talk casual, be long and detailed if they are
												- 38. WHEN IT COMES TO PROPOSALS, BIGGER ISN’T BETTER.
												- 200-400 words
												- succinct “elevator pitch,” followed up by a shortlist of relevant work samples and, if necessary, a brief outline of what you plan to deliver. Always end the proposal by letting them know that you’re available to answer their questions or discuss any aspect of the project further, so if there are any details they want to resolve, they can simply ask you directly.
												- 39. GO FOR VOLUME RATHER THAN PERFECTION.
												- Instead of trying to show potential buyers that I understood their business inside out and upside down, I simply showed them that I had an excellent track record (good feedback, nice portfolio) and was capable of doing an excellent job. Instead of slaving over five spectacular proposals, I would submit 20 simple, straightforward ones. Instead of writing each proposal from scratch, I kept a Word document with a selection of phrases that I could cut and paste into the proposal, depending on the type of job or type of client involved.
												- I’m not telling you to submit identical, one-size-fits-all proposals for every job. In fact, doing so is a direct violation of one of the Elance Terms of Service, and could result in your immediate suspension!
												- 42. DON’T BE AFRAID TO BID HIGH.
												- However, you need to explain to the buyer why the job is worth more to them than they had originally anticipated. The key to doing this successfully is to focus on the value that the buyer will see in hiring a seasoned professional:
												- “As a writer with excellent credentials and five years of experience, I am used to earning upwards of $ 45 per hour for my website content. If you want a writer with my level of expertise, I’m afraid you will need to adjust your budget upwards. As a result, I am submitting a bid that is considerably higher than your stated budget range.”
												- OR: “Your website content is your calling card to the world, and can directly impact your professional image and your ability to win new business. The right web copy is an investment in your business: an investment that can pay off a hundredfold. As a writer with five years of experience and a proven track record of writing content that performs, I can deliver web copy that will convey the kind of confidence and professionalism your company needs to project. Because I believe your project requires the services of a more senior writer to do it justice, I am submitting a bid of $ 750 for the work...”
												- 43. DON’T BAD-MOUTH OTHER CONTRACTORS.
												- it’s never in your best interests to draw the buyer’s attention to the flaws of other providers. It shows that you don’t trust them to make the right choice without your guidance, comes off as controlling, and makes you look petty and negative:
												- NOT THIS: “Unlike some of the other bidders, I’m not some amateur looking to earn some extra pocket money. I’m a full-time, professional writer who takes her profession very seriously.”
												- OR THIS: “Don’t put your project in the hands of one of these writing companies from India. They are known for delivering substandard, barely legible copy. Trust your project to a qualified, native English speaker like me.”
												- BUT THIS: “Your company’s tagline is its touchstone, and you need a marketing-savvy copywriter who knows how to get to the core of your company’s values and aspirations. With a certificate in marketing, five years of copywriting experience, and several great taglines in my portfolio, I have the proven expertise to deliver a tagline that will truly...”
												- 44. DON’T BAD-MOUTH THE BUYER.
												- BUT THIS: “Thank you for providing a link to your current website; it helped me understand your business and your objectives better. There is a lot of great product information contained in the site, and I would love to see it organized in a way that made it easier for your potential customers to find what they needed. If selected for the project, I would begin by...”
												- 45. SPRINKLE A LITTLE SUGAR.
												- A little courtesy and flattery can go a long way
												- Complimenting their business or some aspect of their existing writing materials sets a positive tone and makes the buyer feel good about the project and the role you have to play in it.
												- FOR EXAMPLE: “I reviewed the brochure attached to your job description, and I really like the tagline you chose for your business. I think it does a great job of summing up the benefits you bring your clients, and I would want to build on that tagline when rewriting the brochure...”
												- OR: “Thanks for providing a link to your website. I spent some time looking at it, and I’m intrigued by your software product! What a great idea— I had no idea that this kind of technology was so affordable. You’ve done an excellent job of explaining how it works on the website, and I think the case studies you need written will really help to convey the benefits of TotalSoft to small business customers. As a writer with five years of experience writing for the software industry, I can...”
												- EXTRA TIP: Here’s a great way to hold on to your value, and still meet the price- conscious buyer halfway. Suggest to them that while you can’t deliver that exact project for a reduced fee, you can provide them with a slightly modified version. For instance, if they have asked for a 20-page report to be offered as part of an online squeeze campaign, tell them that you can deliver a more concise 12-page report within their budget, and suggest to them that readers tend to prefer shorter formats on the web. If you can find a creative solution that fits their budget and meets your own earning needs, you both come out winners.
											- Client Communication
												- Never start til money in Escrow
												- Once they respond, give them all contact details - you are then free to provide them with any means of contact you want. Once a buyer has responded to me on Elance, I always sign off on any further communication with my URL, my email address, my local phone number and my Skype ID. This a) allows them to view more information and work samples on my website if they choose and b) shows them that I am always reachable through a range of communication channels.
												- After you finish the very first article, send it to them, and say something along the lines of, "I'm sending this to you now so I can make sure we're headed in the right direction. Please let me know now if there's anything you want different so I can write all the other articles to suit your tastes - since this is one of my first jobs, I really want to win your 5-star feedback, and I want to make sure you get exactly what you're looking for."
												- Note: I do the above with all new clients even later on, though I stop inserting the part about feedback
												- -------
												- Then, when you send the completed articles over, make sure to stress that you're 100% available to make any and all edits needed to get them the articles they want. If you don't think it's laying it on too think (or if you didn't say it in that last message), mention how dedicated you are to 5-star feedback once again.
												- If all this fails and they leave you nothing, you might even send an email over at some point and make sure everything with the articles was okay and if they need edits. Chances are they just forgot at this point and they'll probably be more than happy to jump in there and hook you up if you hooked them up.
												- It probably goes without saying that a) you've delivered the articles earlier than promised and b) you've written higher word counts than promised.
												- Always Overdeliver, Always Be On Time
												- If the client bid for 500 word articles, make sure that none of them are below 500 words. If the client is expecting a one-week delivery time project, bid on completing it in 72 hours. Then complete it in 48 hours. Know what your clients are expecting, then aim to exceed those goals.
												- I communicate constantly, sending updates, samples of the angle I'm taking
												- Follow-up
												- Why Do You Enjoy Being My Customer?
												- What Else Do You Wish My Business Did?
												- Who Should You Tell People/Me About My Business?
												- Send them a letter of appreciation after every job. Hit them up to chat every once in a while.
												- Feedback
												- “A great client who is helpful, understanding, and very clear about project requirements. Highly recommended!”
												- “A great client who was polite and pleasant to work with, very clear about their project requirements, and quick to pay. Highly recommended!”
												- don't get pigeon-holed - "It's easier to get more work out of an existing client than land a new one". Help out their other projects by letting them know your other services (they might forget to check your website)
												- I seek to provide nothing less than five star rated service to clients so if there's anything that would prevent you giving that, please let me know and I'm more than happy to make the necessary adjustments.
												- -
												- Ask existing clients or former for referrals + testimonials (wait after 3-4 projects if you think a client will be a long-term)
											- Elance Improving
												- Elance tips http://bit.ly/1CelbxF
												- Keep improving:
													- Profile
														- Try searching for contractors on Elance yourself, and see how small the images are when they come up in the search results. That’s why most top contractors use a close headshot, either from the shoulders up or cropped even closer.
														- > Need a picture of me in a suit for profile pic
														- triple-check profile description
														- 4. PUMP UP THE VOLUME ON YOUR EMPLOYMENT AND EDUCATION.
														- especially try and get writers courses, webinars or workshops down (free or otherwise)
														- 5. SELECT YOUR TAGLINE STRATEGICALLY.
														- Your tagline needs to sell the single greatest benefit to using your services. What is it that prospective clients are looking for that YOU can provide? Maybe it’s prestige: “Award- winning copywriter.” Maybe it’s results: “I’ll boost your sales by 10%— guaranteed.” Maybe it’s a specialty niche: “Top chef with a flair for food writing.”
														- 7. USE THE “JUMP” TO CREATE A TEASER. (read more part)
														- “I draw on my 10-year career as a writer, my experience as a published business author, and my deep understanding of human psychology to find the right words to solve your toughest communications problems. I have helped small businesses in every sector to find the words that turn readers into customers. How do I do it? Keep reading to discover step one of my never-fail, three-step process. [Read More > >]”
														- OR: “There are many writers on Elance who can do a competent job, producing error-free content that reads well and looks nice on the page. But if you want a writer whose words can actually help you grow your business, there’s just one thing you need to keep in mind. Curious to know what that is? Read on to find out... [Read More > >]”
														- Using an intriguing question or provocative statement to encourage further exploration
														- 8. CREATE A VISUALLY APPEALING PORTFOLIO.
														- writing— viewers probably won’t even be able to read the content within the image. The point is to show them that you have written for high-quality projects. Upload the image to your profile and select it as the default portfolio image.
														- Create folders for the different types of writing your clients may be looking for (“ brochures,” “taglines,” “landing pages,” “email blasts,” etc.), and folders for different sectors (“ healthcare,” “retail,” “ecommerce,” “IT,” etc.). There will obviously be some overlap, and you can place the same samples in different folders (for instance, an IT brochure can be placed in the “IT” sector folder and the “brochure” folder). This type of organization serves two purposes: it makes your portfolio look more populated and well represented, and it helps potential clients find exactly what they need faster.
														- To beef up your portfolio when you don’t have enough client work under your belt, use a venue such as Squidoo.com, Hubpages.com, or Answers.com to create an online showcase for your writing. Go for topic areas that a) you think you would be good at covering, b) are a match for your background and experience, or c) are hot-trending topics that are in demand on Elance. You can determine this last criterion simply by browsing through the job listings, or entering search terms (“ pets,” “legal,” “technical,” etc.) and making a note of terms that yield a high volume of jobs. Just make sure these web pages don’t include any of your direct contact information (email address, phone number, Skype ID, etc.). The Elance terms of service prohibit you from including this information anywhere in your Elance profile.In addition to the screenshot, cut and paste your writing into Word documents, convert them to PDFs and upload the documents to your portfolio.
														- It’s also a good idea to create work samples for the types of jobs you believe you are qualified to undertake.
														- 9. DON’T MAKE IT PERSONAL.
														- Keep the focus on the value you offer as a writer. Every word should be written with the client’s needs in mind: what do they need to know about you in order to decide to trust you with their writing project? Stay away from details unrelated to your clients’ needs— finding a responsible, proven, talented writer. Restrict your overview to the information they want to hear: what writing experience you have, which clients you have worked with, what success you have had in your field, what services you offer, what your specialty areas are, etc.

										- Write a "self-sales letter" for your company
										- But you have to put that shit to the side. Think of an angle. What can you bring to the table that other copywriters can't? What differentiates you? How can you brand yourself in a way that will make other people want to hire you?
										- Go through this, do all of the prep work for a sales letter (figure out your audience, their hopes, fears, your own features and benefits, etc.) and get to fucking work! You may need to write 3 or 4 different ones and test them all against each other--it's okay, testing is a part of effective copywriting (can get into more detail on that if people want me to).
										- Once you have this self-sales letter, you can put it up as your "ad" on various platforms online, and try to drive traffic to it, either through a paid ad or any number of other strategies (ask if you need more detail).
							- _Local businesses:_
								- Contact local advertising agencies
									- I recommend that you start pitching by contacting any advertising agencies in your city, because these places are used to hiring freelance writers and won’t be surpirsed at all at your inquiry. You’re more likely to get a response from these people than from corporate communications departments, so you’ll have better experiences pitching them, which will help build confidence. Approach with a thoughtful cover letter stating your intention (to find freelance writing experience), and include your resume along with a few samples. Try not to be too formal in your approach, because agencies tend to have a fairly “lose” and creative type of atmosphere about them, which they will expect their writers to be able to relate to.

							- Physical portfolio
								- Portfolio - needs good layout, classy folder
								- get it at an ART supply store (not office). 20-24 inches square handled black case, zippered on 3 sides, 15-20 plastic sleeves lined with black paper (allowing you to use both sides) and secured in a multi-ring binder.
								- $80-100 - look under www.dickblick.com - presentation cases not portfolios
								- you can even mount samples on loose pieces of stiff black cardboard (mounting/matte board)
							- Don't do spec (free) work

						- Types of copywriting gigs
							- _3 major types_
								- B2C
								- B2B
								- IC (internal communications)
									- white papers, training materials, product descriptions, press releases
								- _Important differences_
									- B2C+B2B is public-facing so I can use those samples without asking permission first
							- End Users or Middle Men (producing project for EU)
								- End Users
									- Pros
										- + They're used to working with MMs premium rates so will be happy with yours
										- + No MMs so one less layer of stakeholders and bureaucracy to slow down my projects
									- Cons
										- Small ones require more educating about marketing e.g. 1/3 deposit is standard
											- they're used to doing all marketing in-house
								- Middlemen
									- Pros/Cons
										- Pros
											- They know your role so give you time researching and they know fair costs
										- Cons
											- Extra layer means more project editing sometimes
											- May restrict contact with EU, which can make it difficult to get vital research questions answered
											- They will often do final edits to your finished product
											- They may try to beat you down on price
									- Don't go direct to EU unless you've asked MM if it's fine - sometimes they see you as 'their copywriter'
							- _Many different types of projects_
								- Ad campaigns
								- annual reports,

							- CV
								- Improve resume - hard numbers + name dropping, no adjectives just facts
								- Feel free to include some international education e.g. MBA in X country
								- think like a hiring manger, tell them what they need to hear.
								- Always write a short cover letter, and as appropriate, adjust job titles to match what you’re applying for. Don’t just canvass like a lazy fuck, you have to TARGET. As extra juice, I like to stagger my experience to always build UP, up the little corporate ladder. Have it make sense. Every “job” you had, make sure it hits a two year mark. No gaps (reliable)
								- You have to make them want you. The only way to make them want you is for them to feel that you got options. It’s just like pickup. Of course I knew lots of jargon and was super personable the rest of the time, just throwing in a little edge once in a while to make sure I’m not looking desperate.

								- (company compliment)
								- About me:
								- Web copywriting for more than 5 years including sales letters, email newsletters, web copy and articles
								- All my work life has been freelance so I know the importance of submitting work by deadlines
								- My personal blog is Habit Laboratory where you can see some article-writing ability and I've attached a few to this email for ease (all SEO-optimised targeting one keyword)
								- My most recent client was an eCommerce business in the health vertical, you can see the work I did for them here *link - http://aaronsollesse.com/ap-copy* (sales letter, banner ads, email newsletters etc)
								- Degree in Bsc Psychology
								- You can see my professional website here (http://aaronsollesse.com) and my blog Habit Laboratory here (http://habitlaboratory.com)
								- I know that my skills would be an ideal fit for your company so I look forward to hearing from you.
								- If you have any more questions please don't hesitate to ask.
								- Regards
								- (reply from AaronSollesse.com address)

							- Elevator pitch
								- My personal favorite handle or moniker for what I did when I was working was...
								- Creative Marketing Professional or, when calling on small businesses for some quick and easy money was...
								- Independent Marketing Analyst...and a free 150 offer to do a quick assessment of where their ad dollars were going...what the cost of a new customer, called acquisition and the lifetime value of the customer...figuring out the LTV can tell you how much to spend to acquire a customer.
								- Call yourself something different. What does a creative marketing professional do? Anything and everything needed to get the bottom line to fatten up into the black side of business life. Which includes writing copy as needed.
								- Write a PERSONAL LETTER with name of business owner, I liked to use square thank you cards, something different.
								- I'd THANK them for their time and attention. And I would call myself a REMOTE SALESMAN. Every business guy knows what a salesman is.
								- Remote salesman?? What is that? You simply tell the business person you are able to use your computer, which is remote from his business and MAKE SALES AND INCREASE HIS PROFITS...AND IF YOU HAVE THE BALLS...MAKE it a no cost, no expense offer.
								- OR a trial test.
								- Have a PLAN. HOW will you help this business get new customers?
								- What is his LTV of his customer? Or is it a one off deal?
								- Would a postcard mailing in his zip code work? Direct mail? TV? Radio? Coupon Clipper? Rewards card? Referral program??
								- HOW can YOU, as a remote salesperson, help any business make more money and what exactly will you do? Tell them you will write copy...or you are a copywriter...and they just don't get the value you bring to the table.
								- * 'Thank you for your time and attention. There are 3 simple things that you can do to your ad (enclosed) that could double or perhaps triple your response. If you want to know what they are, give me a call at xxx-xxx-xxxx. I'd also like to offer you a Free Marketing Evaluation (I normally charge $197). If you'd like to take me up on my offer, give me a call at xxx-xxx-xxxx. John Russell - Independent Ad Writer/Marketing Professional xxx-xxx-xxxx'
								- NOW, when I was assessing marketing, I KNEW what the prices are for other media...like 495 for half page ad in coupon clipper...995 for shoutouts on radio, or 1495 for a 30 second tv on local access or something like that...
								- IF YOU CALL yourself a professional, then you should act like one.
								- Advice 2...start with the coupon clippers, local weeklies where local businesses advertise week after week, month after month...
								- KNOW how much these ads cost. Write a letter or cold call and tell him what you can do for him...I like letters, if I think I'm a copywriter, then I think I can write a persuasive letter to at least get an interview...eh?

								- Lack of portfolio shouldn't hold you back on promoting your business. It can even be a reason why you're running a special deal. Plenty of copywriters have used that marketing angle when they started (I used a variation of it).
								- [jobsearch.about.com](http://jobsearch.about.com/) - learn how to create a good resume
								- Persuasive writer - get them to buy, join your email list, whatever
								- I recommend you create you own list of companies that demonstrate they pay for marketing and advertising yet they are not big enough to pay ad agencies. These companies tend to be between $3 million and $30 million in revenue.
								- I would not target your local small businesses since they do not have the money for copywriter services. Yes, they need it. But they don't recognize this fact and they won't pay for it. You'll get bloodied real fast marketing to these businesses...avoid them like the plague.
								- Look for jobs with:
									- long contract
									- REPUTABLE and your services make a big difference to their bottom line
									- they have worked with writers
									- the work is interesting
									- pay per article
								- Don't take on jobs that
									- Ask for free trials
									- incentive-based pay
									- non-existent/private sites
									- blog networks/SEO/mass article writing work
									- pay per hour (limits earning potential, damages productivity + clouds clients judgement i.e. they won't be impressed by quick turnaround or efficiency of work)
								- GOALS
								- - Seek referrals
								- They want to more value out of you than it costs to hire you - how are you going to make them money
								- Effective immediately, insist on getting paid at least 50% upfront before you spend a minute on a client's project. If they can't pay you 50% upfront, then they're probably not going be able to pay your full fee once you complete the project. 50% deposit (I make it a non-refundable deposit) is for booking the project on your schedule of paying client work
								- No harm in sending a proposal, could land a valuable client
								- Where is the most likely location going to be where my ideal pool of clients are amassing in any great numbers?
								- Where are they hanging out?
								- What are they searching for?
								- Which websites or forums do they belong to?
								- What are they looking for precisely online?
								- What other complimentary interests might they have to tap directly into what you know you can offer them with the skills in your possession?
								- Have a look at the Warrior Special Offers and the For Hire section - see what others charge - then decide what you want.
								- You don't have to state a "set" fee on your "pitch" letter.
								- You can entice clients by saying you are extremely "cost effective"
								- You're aiming to make them substantially more money than they'll ever pay you.
								- Doing this and not mentioning a fee can create a high level of curiosity - which is good.
								- When you discuss the "assignment" work out the time you'll spend.
								- And quote your fee (based on the research, writing, editing, and what you are genuinely worth).
								- You could reduce it - (but be careful - you'll soon see just how long it can take to write excellent copy).
								- And say it's a "special discount"
								- Because you are "new"
								- But reinforce this by saying you'll always be trying harder to bring them fabulous results.
								- Or don't give any discount but still say "I'll be trying so much harder to get you the best response"
								- how the hell do you know it'll only take you a week to write MY copy?
								- HUH?
								- It might take you that long just to interview me, research my market, hone in upon my brand, niche down my audience and position my product in just the right way.
								- Suppose you're a really fast copywriter and can do a client project in 8 hours. That's research, writing, editing, proofing, and polishing the salesletter in a day -- even on topics that you're not already familiar with.
								- Do this - I charge $197 for WSO copy and that's a freakin' bargin! With every gig I land the price on the next gig goes up $50
								- I write online sales pages
								- I write online ads
								- I write mobile ads
								- I write sales emails
								- I write e-newsletters
								- I write home pages
								- I write product pages
								- Several years experience in copywriting for email marketing, sales letters and launch campaigns. Also experienced in SEO practices
								- Creative and academic writing
								- copywriting for SEO, ad copy for paid search campaigns, landing page copy
								- Recommendations
									- Here’s what some of my clients had to say about my service…
									- “I would very highly recommend Northword Writing and Jeff for any project involved with writing content material. I would rate them 5+++++++.”
									- “Outstanding professionalism and very fast service!”
								- Profile description
									- Don’t spend days on your description, because most clients will judge you based on what you write in your proposal first. Still, spend at least an hour or so crafting a strong message that allows potential clients to get to know you a bit more.
									- Your description should do several things:
										- · Catch attention.
										- · Immediately point out your strongest points.
										- · Identify yourself as credible.
										- · Build value in you as a freelancer.
										- · Distinguish you from anyone else.
									- Before beginning to write your description, it’ll help to sit down and come up with a list of your accomplishments. These can be anything that make you sound more credible. You can use these parts of your description in your profile, as well as in your proposals.
									- If you don’t have previous professional writing experience, try to come up with anything that’ll improve how credible you sound. For example:
									- · If you had straight A’s in English
									- · If you have debate experience
									- · If you’ve been published in your college newspaper (you could say you’re a published author)
									- · If you’ve trained people in previous jobs
									- · If you’ve worked for prestigious companies
									- · Previous client or employer testimonials or comments. Even if it’s not about writing, but about your character – E.g. “Jane is one of the most hardworking people I’ve ever met.”
									- · If you’ve studied literature for personal enjoyment
									- · If you’ve ever been featured in a paper or local radio – Again, doesn’t have to be writing related. Just helps build an image of credibility.
									- If you really truly can’t write a description that builds credibility, you can always just undercut all the first world country writers. You’ll have to work harder for less money, just to build credibility. Do that for your first $500 and you’ll then be able to use your experience to increase your bids up to the market rates (and beyond.)
									- Note that your profile in the beginning can just be a couple of paragraphs long. Work on it and add to it over time.
									- It also helps to look at high paid contractors and their profiles. I noticed that a lot of them had nothing fancy.
									- Add all of your academic information with your major and relevant coursework.
									- Add each of your previous work experiences, your responsibilities, and how you added value to the company for each of these placements.
									- You can easily add “author” to your name. Just write an ebook — it only has to be 20-30 pages on a topic you know about. If it’s in the niche you specialize in in writing, then all the better. Voila! You’re an author.
									- [http://72tips.com/2011/how-to-become-an-award-winning-writer/](http://72tips.com/2011/how-to-become-an-award-winning-writer/)
							- Tips
								- -growth hacker?
								- In B2B (improving a business' marketing) emotional, high-pressure sales tactics may be inappropriate (because these aren't discretionary purchases, they're essential for growth)
								- don't 'sell', establish a win-win situation because you're their partner e.g. I'm not even sure my service is a fit for your company, but I'd certainly love the opportunity to learn more about what you do and see if there is some common ground.
								- show you understand them and their mindset
									- show them through stories + evidence that you can provide VALUE
								- sell the meeting, not the service - getting together to explore what you both do and whether there's a fit (if there's a need)
								- Call to Action with specific next step at end of meeting e.g. follow up call at set time+day, another meeting
								- Don't have a scarcity mindset - money is abundant and you should only sell if they need my help
							- Answer the question 'why you' - USP
							- writing skill - benefits - why should they care? Matters even if you're trying to get promoted or a client biz promoted
							- USP - needs to be a gap in the market, something you consistently fulfill, specialise, create a tagline/unique job title
							- if you're repeatedly hired, ask what the special trait/value you provide is
							- ((644c0a7b-fea6-458e-93d3-33bcf1166ef0))
								- 1/3rd deposit is standard, or 1/2 upfront

							- Elance has always been my bread and butter, but it it's a very time-intensive marketing channel unless you hire a bidder. Other things I'm working on right now include
								- Emailing companies directly. Have had success in the past. Yesterday I sent out one query and got one response - imagine that. Wink
								- Blogging. Just finished my site redesign to focus more heavily on copy specifically and blog is ready to roll. The blog is not only great for traffic but is a huge cred-builder with clients I meet elsewhere. I had one client email me to say I was a "mind magician" because one of my articles hooked him so well. He said he went to the site to see my portfolio and spent two hours reading. Nice.
								- LinkedIn. Beefing up my profile and will be sharing my articles there. I get some VERY SOLID prospects hitting me up with messages there sometimes.
								- Paid advertising. This will be new territory for me, but some of my best copywriting clients are KILLING IT. I expect a learning curve, but I aim to reverse engineer what they're doing. (Note: I'm focusing more on PPC campaigns, but if anyone has an engaged high-traffic site they think would be interested in my services, I might be willing to try that too. PM me.)

						- Closing:
					- In-person
						- need a business card, possibly nice letterhead + envelopes in future + carry portfolio to all meetings (need a backpack/briefcase/suit?)
						- business card needs to say 'writer' or 'writing' on it (my main income avenue)
					- Client analysis
						- small jobs often lead to big jobs
						- Embrace weird and potentially off-putting projects, you may learn something useful (+ get paid)
				- _Once client found:_
					- Project discussion
						- Be willing to fight for what's best for a project in the face of a client's revisions
						- research the topic thoroughly, ask loads of questions,

					- Client induction questionnaire
						- Client qs
							- http://www.copyblogger.com/copywriter-client-questions/
							- Followup:
							- This is my proposed figure X
							- This includes 2 rounds of rewriting after I've sent the draft work to you and additional edits will cost extra.
							- Let's determine a /The time limit for this project will be X date, by which point all work is considered finished and final payment is due.
							- Final Payment: The full 100% of project fee is expected no later than 15 calendar days from the date of submittance.
							- --
							- Questions to ensure copy is perfect for their biz
								- Question #1
								- Do you already have a design and layout prepared, or will you be handing my copy to the designer? (If you already have a template picked out for the piece, I’ll need to see it so that I can write copy that fits the template in terms of the word count and special text elements such as headers, sidebars, pull-quotes, and so on.)
								- Question #2
								- Do you have measurable goals you wish to achieve with this communications piece? If so, how will you be measuring its success?
								- Question #3
								- If the material will be placed in a specific context, what is that context? For instance, if it’s a print ad or advertorial, what magazine will it be featured in?
								- Question #4
								- Have you developed any brand elements for your company? (These may include key messages, unique selling propositions, key differentiators, competitor analyses, taglines, audience profiles, visual brand creative brief, tone-of-voice document, etc. If you haven’t developed any of these core elements or spent time thinking strategically about your image and market position, we may want to take a step back and create a brand strategy document before progressing on to specific communications pieces.)
								- Question #5
								- Are there any existing external marketing or internal positioning materials I can take a look at? (This can include a website, brochures, case studies, business plans, strategy documents, etc.)
								- Question #6
								- Who is the intended audience for this piece? Do they represent a distinct demographic? (Age, gender, ethnicity, earning power, employment status, education level, geographic location, family status, etc.) What do you know about their psychographics (attitudes, opinions, interests, beliefs)?
								- Question #7
								- What are the pain points for this audience? How does your product/service address those pain points?
								- Question #8
								- Is your target audience the key decision maker when it comes to taking action? Or do they have a boss/spouse/business partner/other they will need to convince before they can move forward? If so, what motivates that other decision-maker?
								- Question #9
								- Do you know how to get a response from this audience? Have you done something in the past that has been particularly successful in eliciting a response? Have you tried something that specifically failed to reach them?
								- What are you happy with in your current communications? What do you think needs to change?
								- Question #10
								- Do you know which communication channels your audience prefers? For instance, are they heavy social media users? Do they prefer print media?
								- Question #11
								- At what stage in the customer/client relationship will your audience be receiving this piece? Is the piece the equivalent of a cold call to a stranger? Or will the recipient know who you are? If they know who you are, have they shown an interest in your products/services before? At what point in the sales funnel would you place this audience?
								- Question #12
								- For web copy: Where will prospects have heard of you before they reach your website? Will they have found you through search results? Will they have heard of you through word-of-mouth? Will they have attended a presentation/webinar? Clicked through on an email message?
								- Question #13
									- What is the comprehension level for this audience? If yours is a new or complicated or technologically advanced product/service, how familiar will the audience be with it? Will they understand or need to know the technical details?
								- Question #14
									- What is the most compelling benefit that will motivate your prospects to learn more or try your product? What are the top five benefits?
								- Question #15
									- For business-to-business materials: What size of business are you targeting? Sole proprietors/freelancers? Microbusinesses? Small- to medium-sized businesses? Enterprises?
								- Question #16
									- For business-to-business materials: Are there specific sectors/industries/verticals you think would be more likely to use your product/service, or that have expressed more of an interest in it?
								- Question #17
									- Who are your direct competitors? (These are companies occupying the same space you do and offering a very similar solution.) What differentiates you from your competitors? Have you developed competitive analyses or matrixes to compare your offering to theirs?
								- Question #18
									- Who are your indirect competitors? (These are companies offering a different solution to the same problem. For instance, a fitness center’s competitors might include home fitness equipment, fitness DVDs, and dance classes as well as other fitness centers.)
								- Question #19
									- Will your audience be aware of your direct and indirect competitors? Will they have conducted research on the different solutions to their problem? Or are they likely to be hearing about this type of solution for the first time?
								- Question #20
									- Are there any misperceptions about your company, product or service that need to be addressed and dispelled?
								- Question #21
									- Can I take a closer look at your product/service? If it’s a physical product, can you send me a sample? If it’s software, can I download and use it? If it’s a membership, can you grant me temporary access?
								- Question #22
									- Do you have any customer testimonials that can be integrated into the material? If not, is it possible to collect some? Are there customers I could talk to?
								- Question #23
									- Are there specific copy styles you have seen recently that you really like, and that you think would be a good fit for your company/product/service? Provide examples and describe what you like about them.
								- Question #24
									- Are there copy styles that you specifically don’t like and feel would not be appropriate for your purposes? Provide examples and describe what you don’t like about them.
						- Have them help answer the _Project Analysis_ questions

					- My Pricing
						- Calculate value of prospecting time e.g. 100 hours prospecting time = $10k work, thus prospecting time = $100/hr
						- How Much To Charge?
							- "Prices don't go up over time... they go up over results."
							- - Vin Montello
								- MARKETING
									- Do more writing courses + have them verified
									- Write and guest blog e.g. returnofkings.com, mattforney, 3rd millenium men
								- writing copy for imaginary businesses. Or better yet, write copy for real businesses (for free) and offer it to them as a sample
									- made-up pieces, or pieces you wrote for companies that never actually hired you to write anything for them.
								- publish your portfolio online. The website “Writer’s Residence” offers a simple portfolio template you can upload to a web server pretty easily: [http://writersresidence.com/](http://writersresidence.com/)
							- seo/content writing
							- on the lower end I work for 25$ - 30$ an hour (been at it 2 years). [after 2 years he makes minimum $2500-3600] 1st year I was making $1500/mo part-time whilst studying for university. full time you can realistically expect 2.500$ a month... and by fulltime I mean about 25-30 hours a week, because seriously who wants to work all the time?
							- you should be able to get at least 15-20$ an hour pretty much right off the bat!
							- Give me $35,000 and 10% of your gross sales and I'll write an ad for you
							- A good copywriter shouldn't cost you any money - they'll make YOU lots of money (we're an investment).
							- Need to be reaching at least $100/£65 per hour
							- aim for a minimum of $100 per billable hour - Beyond Border's posts
							- At the start, though, you'll be doing a lot of "hourly rate conversion." This consists of estimating how many hours a project will take, then extrapolating that out times your hourly rate. You may have to lowball a few projects when you're starting out, but I wouldn't recommend starting any lower than $30/hour, and generously allocate your time. Don't be afraid to ask a client how much they've budgeted for a project, either--you'd be surprised at how much some clients will pay for something relatively straightforward.
							- Once you're making $50/hour and above, you can make a very nice income working just 20 hours a week--at least enough to live on. That leaves more time to game chicks, work on other side hustles, create and promote your own products, etc. And with each project, you're effectively decreasing the "hours" portion of the equation (since you should always bill by the project whenever possible), which "makes" you more time over the long run.
							- [http://www.writersmarket.com/assets/pdf/How_Much_Should_I_Charge.pdf](http://www.writersmarket.com/assets/pdf/How_Much_Should_I_Charge.pdf)
